name: Bayfront Park & Marina
blurb: Downtown's front porch on the bay — sailboats, a splash fountain, and the best sunset photo in the city framed by the Ringling Bridge.
tip: Walk the bridge at golden hour — the unofficial test-drive for downtown condo life.

name: Lido Beach & Pavilion
blurb: Siesta's quieter sibling, five minutes from St. Armands Circle. Wide white sand, a classic mid-century pavilion with a pool and concession stand, and far fewer crowds. This is the beach you walk to from dinner on the Circle.
tip: Luxury travelers: book sunset at a Circle restaurant, then walk the sand after. It's the lifestyle preview for every St. Armands and Lido condo buyer.

name: Greer Island — "Beer Can Island"
blurb: Longboat's wild north tip: a driftwood 'boneyard' beach where weathered trees lie bleached on the sand and a walkable sandbar arcs into Longboat Pass. Reachable on foot from the Broadway access at low tide, or by boat — locals raft up on the pass side.
tip: Go at low tide for the full sandbar. This is the photo that makes people move here.

name: Marie Selby Botanical Gardens
blurb: Bayfront gardens famous for orchids and epiphytes, plus a banyan grove and a tree-canopy walk. A green exhale minutes from downtown.
tip: The downtown campus and the Spanish Point campus to the south are both worth it.

name: Mote Marine Aquarium
blurb: Sharks, manatees, and sea-turtle research on City Island — a genuine working marine lab, not just a tourist tank.
tip: A new mainland campus is coming near I-75; the current City Island site's future is fun to speculate about.

name: Sarasota Farmers Market
blurb: 70+ vendors take over downtown every Saturday morning — produce, flowers, prepared food, and live music. The week's best free morning out.
tip: Saturdays 7am–1pm. Grab a coffee, do a loop, then walk Main Street while it's quiet.

name: Sarasota Opera House
blurb: A restored 1926 Mediterranean Revival gem anchoring the downtown arts district — a big part of why Sarasota punches above its weight culturally.
tip: The arts calendar is a genuine value driver downtown; it's the first thing relocating buyers fall for.

name: Siesta Beach (Main Public Beach)
blurb: The famous one — 99% pure quartz sand that stays cool underfoot, repeatedly ranked the #1 beach in America (Dr. Beach 2011 & 2017, TripAdvisor's U.S. #1). Year-round lifeguards, 800+ free parking spaces, playground, concessions, tennis and volleyball.
tip: In season, be in the lot before 10am or ride the free Siesta Key Breeze trolley. Families: the lifeguarded zones and shallow shelf here are the safest swim on the Key.

name: South Lido / Ted Sperling Park
blurb: Four bodies of water meet at Lido's south tip — Gulf, Big Pass, Brushy Bayou and Sarasota Bay. Kayak the mangrove tunnels (guided tours launch here), picnic under Australian pines, and watch boats run the pass with Siesta Key across the water.
tip: The mangrove-tunnel kayak tour is the single best family adventure on the Keys. Swimmers: stay clear of Big Pass — the current is real.

name: St. Armands Circle
blurb: An open-air ring of boutiques, galleries, and restaurants between downtown and Lido Beach. Equal parts shopping trip and people-watching.
tip: Living within a golf-cart ride of the Circle is its own micro-market — ask me about it.

name: The Ringling
blurb: John Ringling's 66-acre bayfront estate: a world-class art museum, the Cà d'Zan mansion, and a circus museum. The single best rainy-day plan in town.
tip: Mondays the art galleries are free. Give yourself half a day — people always underestimate it.

name: Van Wezel Performing Arts Hall
blurb: The unmistakable purple seashell on the bayfront — Broadway tours, concerts, and comedy with the bay shimmering through the lobby glass.
tip: The surrounding bayfront is slated for major redevelopment — a long-game reason to watch nearby addresses.

name: C’est La Vie
blurb:
C'est La Vie is an authentic French bakery and café that feels like a slice of Paris in the heart of downtown Sarasota. It's a quintessential spot for breakfast, known for amazing croissants, fresh crepes, and traditional French flair served from early morning onward. Enjoy a relaxed café vibe as downtown Sarasota wakes up around you.

name: Drift Kitchen & Bar - Lido Beach Resort (8th floor)
address: 700 Benjamin Franklin Dr, Sarasota, FL 34236, USA
blurb:
At Drift Kitchen & Bar you'll find elevated Gulf-view dining perched on the 8th floor of Lido Beach Resort, offering panoramic, floor-to-ceiling views over Lido Beach and Sarasota's coastline. The menu highlights fresh seafood, house-made pastas, brick-oven pizzas, and contemporary American dishes that blend coastal flavors with a refined, resort-style touch. But in the end, you're here for the views and the resort feel.

Open to the public

name: Owens Fish Camp
blurb:
Low-country seafood + backyard vibe. The Fish Camp in historic Burns Court delivers laid-back Old-Florida charm with great local seafood and Southern comfort favorites. Anchored by its iconic banyan, it blends coastal flavors, history, and a lively front-porch fish-camp vibe. Great pies and "boozy" milkshakes to end your night.
No reservations - busy on the weekends so plan for a drink and live music in their backyard while you wait.

Food style: Seafood & Southern comfort.

name: Siesta Promenade (New Benderson Dev - 2025?)
blurb:
Estimated 2025 for retail and apartments, then hotel after.The project will comprise a total of 10 retail and restaurant buildings, including a yet-to-be-named grocer. It will have 140,000 square feet of retail space, with a minimum of 7,000 square feet of office space, and an apartment building with 414 units, 25 of which will be affordable. Siesta Promenade will also include an 80-foot high, 130-room hotel, which will be larger than any single hotel on nearby Siesta Key, where space for such a dense hotel is limited.
https://www.sarasotamagazine.com/home-and-real-estate/2023/09/benderson-siesta-promenade-south-sarasota-development

name: SOTA Hotel & Residences (FUTURE)
blurb:
Breaking ground summer 2023SOTA will be 16 stories high and have luxury residences, hotel suites and a 4,500-square-foot ground-level restaurant open to the public.
SOTA will have 35 condominium residences spanning six floors, 120 hotel rooms managed by Tapestry Collection by Hilton, and a 4,500-square-foot restaurant on the ground floor.
Residential condos at SOTA range from $1.7 million to $5 million. For comparison, nearby luxury condo buildings, like The Edge, which is scheduled to break ground by April, start at $2.6 million, while condos at the newly finished The Collection, start at $2 million-and only one unit remains available.

address: 301 Bird Key Dr, Sarasota, FL 34236, USA
blurb:
Full Membership (all the same):
- $20,000 one time
- $9,400 /yr (couples)
- $1,500/yr Food & Bev minimumSlip Rates (Currently waitlisted):
- $13.25/ft in water or $18.75/ft on lift
- 45' in water slips and 14,000 lb lift caps-Social club (dining Tues-Sun ala carte, brunch on the weekends), Bocce, Tennis, Pool
Our Club's reconstructed 42-slip marina offers 21 boat lifts from 10,000-14,000-pound capacity, 21 in-water slips that can accommodate sail and power vessels from 28 to 50 feet, and a kayak lift. The marina also offers diesel fuel and gasoline, along with complete pump-out facilities. Complimentary Wi-Fi is available at all slips. The marina is staffed full-time by experienced team members prepared to meet all members' boating needs. Marina staff monitors VHF channel 16.

name: West of the Trail
blurb: Historic neighborhoods West of the Trail, convenient to...

name: Siesta Beach Access 5 (Village)
address: Ocean Blvd at Avenida Navarra, Siesta Key
blurb: The village beach access — a short barefoot walk from Siesta Key Village's restaurants and shops straight onto the quartz sand. Small free lot plus street spots.
tip: Lot fills by 9am in season. Park once in the Village, hit the beach, then walk to lunch — best car-free day on the Key.

name: Crescent Beach & Point of Rocks (Access 12)
address: Point of Rocks Rd, Siesta Key
blurb: Crescent Beach's south end hides Point of Rocks — limestone outcroppings with the best snorkeling on the Key. Calmer, less crowded sand than the main beach.
tip: Bring water shoes and go at slack tide for clear snorkeling. Street parking is scarce — arrive early or bike down.

name: Turtle Beach Park
blurb: The Key's laid-back south-end beach: darker, coarser sand famous for shelling, a shaded playground, picnic shelters, volleyball, and a public boat ramp onto Little Sarasota Bay. Rarely crowded.
tip: Best shelling after a storm. The adjacent Turtle Beach Campground is the sleeper way to test-drive island life.

name: North Lido Beach
address: North end of Benjamin Franklin Dr, Lido Key
blurb: Lido's secluded stretch — no lifeguards, no concessions, just quiet white sand and sea oats a ten-minute walk from St. Armands Circle. The locals' escape hatch.
tip: Strong currents near New Pass — walk it, wade it, but save the real swimming for the lifeguarded main beach.

name: Broadway Beach Access (Longboat North)
blurb: The main public doorway to Longboat's north-end sand and the walk out to Greer Island. Real parking lot (rare on Longboat), short boardwalk, miles of uncrowded beach in both directions.
tip: This is the access locals use. Whitney Beach's wide, empty stretch runs south from here.

name: Mid-Key Beach Accesses (Longboat)
address: Gulf of Mexico Dr — General Harris St / Neptune Ave / Mayfield St, Longboat Key
blurb: Longboat keeps its beaches quiet by design: a string of small, marked accesses off Gulf of Mexico Drive (General Harris, Neptune, Mayfield, Gulfside Rd) with a handful of spaces each — and essentially private Gulf frontage once you're on the sand.
tip: If the first access is full, the next is a minute away. This scarcity of parking is exactly why Longboat beaches feel private — and why owners love it.

name: Bayfront Park (Longboat Key)
address: 4052 Gulf of Mexico Dr, Longboat Key
blurb: Longboat's family hub on the bay side: playground, splash pad, kayak launch, tennis and pickleball, dog parks, and picnic lawns looking across Sarasota Bay.
tip: Launch a kayak an hour before sunset and paddle the bay side — calm water, mangroves, and skyline views.

name: Quick Point Nature Preserve
address: 100 Gulf of Mexico Dr, Longboat Key (south tip)
blurb: 34 acres of trails and boardwalks over lagoons and mangroves at Longboat's south tip, with New Pass and the Lido skyline across the water. Ospreys, herons, and the occasional manatee in the lagoons.
tip: Pair it with lunch across the bridge — the preserve sits at the foot of the New Pass bridge to Lido.

name: Bobby Jones Golf Club
blurb: Sarasota's 1926 Donald Ross municipal, reborn: architect Richard Mandell's restoration reopened in December 2023 and Golf Digest promptly ranked it the #2 Best New or Remodeled Affordable Course in America (2024). 18 restored Ross holes plus The Gillespie 9-hole short course, wrapped in a 300-acre nature park.
tip: Walk it like it's 1926 — firm, fast, and the best golf value between Tampa and Naples. Book well ahead in season.

name: The Meadows Golf Club
blurb: Big 2026 news: the former private Meadows Country Club is now a 36-hole PUBLIC destination under Arnold Palmer Golf Management (opened to public play June 2026). The Meadows course (Ron Garl redesign) and the Arthur Hills-designed Groves course, 15 minutes from downtown.
tip: Newly public means tee times are still easy to get. That window will close.

name: University Park Country Club
blurb: 27 Ron Garl-designed holes consistently rated among the best public-accessible golf in Southwest Florida — nearly five stars on GolfPass and a four-star Golf Digest 'Places to Play' fixture since 1996. Semi-private: open to daily-fee play with a 3-day public booking window.
tip: Any two of the three nines make a par-72. Course conditions here embarrass most private clubs.

name: Legacy Golf Club at Lakewood Ranch
blurb: An Arnold Palmer Signature design and one of the area's most-reviewed public courses — 4.2/5 on GolfPass across 1,100+ golfer reviews. Generous fairways, Palmer-signature risk-reward par 5s.
tip: Play early — afternoon wind makes the water carries honest.

name: IMG Academy Golf Club
blurb: The 1972 El Conquistador course, now home base for IMG Academy's junior golf program. Semi-private and open to public play — par 72, 73.9 rating / 135 slope from the tips, rolling terrain over Sarasota Bay bluffs you won't find elsewhere in flat Florida.
good_for: A real test · watch future tour pros
tip: The back-nine bay views are the area's best-kept golf secret.

name: Longboat Key Club — Links on Longboat
address: 301 Gulf of Mexico Dr, Longboat Key
blurb: The Gulf-side course at the island's legendary club — redesigned by Ron Garl in 2014, 6,748 yards threading lagoons and sea breeze with the Gulf over your shoulder. Private club, but playable: resort guests at The Resort at Longboat Key Club can book tee times (24 hours ahead). A Golf Digest 'Best Places to Play' honoree.
tip: Stay one night at the Resort, play the next morning — the legal cheat code into one of Florida's great private clubs. Members get 45 holes; ask Ryan what membership looks like with a Longboat address.

name: Longboat Key Club — Harbourside
blurb: 27 tree-lined holes on the bay side of Longboat Key Club — the Red Hawk, White Egret and Blue Heron nines mix and match into three distinct 18s, winding through Sarasota Bay mangroves and the Moorings marina. Private; resort guests can play.
tip: Blue Heron at dusk, bay on three sides — this is the view that sells Harbourside condos.

name: Sara Bay Country Club
blurb: Donald Ross, 1926 — the same golden-age pedigree as Bobby Jones, kept private. Bobby Jones himself and the era's stars played exhibitions here. Classic small greens, old oaks, timeless routing minutes north of downtown.

name: Palm Aire Country Club
blurb: 36 holes across the Champions and Lakes courses at one of the area's established member clubs, ten minutes from downtown near UTC. A perennial choice for buyers who want two courses and a full club calendar without island pricing.

name: TPC Prestancia
blurb: The TPC network's Sarasota outpost in gated Palmer Ranch — 36 holes including the Stadium Course that hosted the Senior PGA Tour's Chrysler Cup. Private and prestigious, with membership tied closely to the surrounding Prestancia community.
vibe: TPC Stadium Course · Senior PGA host
tip: Homes inside Prestancia's gates come with a direct line to membership — ask Ryan for what's available.

name: Laurel Oak Country Club
blurb: 36 championship holes behind the gates of one of Sarasota's premier golf communities — the Gary Player-designed East course and Ron Garl's West course, on 813 oak-canopied acres.
vibe: Gary Player East · Ron Garl West

name: The Founders Club
blurb: Robert Trent Jones Jr. carved this one through 700 acres of old-Florida oak hammocks east of downtown — routinely named among Florida's best residential courses. Intimate membership, tournament-grade conditioning.

name: Serenoa Golf Club
blurb: A friendly public 18 south of Clark Road: tight, tree-lined fairways, plenty of water, and more sandhill cranes than golf carts. Honest golf at an honest price.

name: Rosedale Golf & Country Club
blurb: A semi-private Ted McAnlis 18 in a gated community on the Sarasota–Manatee line. Limited outside play keeps the course uncrowded; the club vibe is relaxed rather than stuffy.

name: Peridia Golf & Country Club
blurb: A semi-private executive 18 — par 3s and short 4s you can walk in under three hours. The right speed for family golf, beginners, or a quick morning round before the beach.

name: Manatee County Golf Course
blurb: The area's beloved county muni — a well-kept, walkable public 18 fifteen minutes north of Longboat's north bridge. Where the locals actually play.
